clinicalStem: A 26-year-old primigravida at 39 weeks of gestation is admitted in active labour. On vaginal examination at 08:00 her cervix is 4 cm dilated; abdominal palpation shows 4/5 of the fetal head palpable above the symphysis pubis. These findings are plotted on the partograph. A repeat vaginal examination is performed at 12:00 and reveals 5 cm dilatation with 3/5 head palpable. The cervical dilatation plot has now crossed to the right of the alert line. Fetal heart rate is 138 bpm and maternal observations are normal. What is the most appropriate immediate action?
options: Commence oxytocin augmentation immediately
Prepare for emergency caesarean section
Refer the mother to a higher-level facility for further evaluation
Repeat vaginal examination in 30 minutes
Perform artificial rupture of membranes and reassess in 4 hours
explanation: When cervical dilatation plots cross to the right of the alert line on the partograph, the recommended response is to refer the mother to a higher-level facility for further evaluation and possible intervention. The alert line serves as an early warning that labour may be deviating from normal, triggering transfer before the situation becomes critical. Immediate oxytocin or caesarean section is premature at the alert-line stage; those decisions are made after specialist assessment.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Oxytocin augmentation is not the immediate response to alert-line crossing; it requires specialist assessment and exclusion of cephalopelvic disproportion first.
Emergency caesarean section is not indicated at the alert-line stage; the partograph has not reached the action line and fetal and maternal parameters are currently normal.
Correct. Crossing the alert line is the signal to refer for further evaluation at a higher facility; this allows specialist input before labour becomes obstructed.
Repeating the vaginal examination in 30 minutes does not address the slow progress and would waste time that could be used for appropriate referral.
Artificial rupture of membranes and waiting a further 4 hours does not follow the partograph protocol; once the alert line is crossed, referral is indicated.
clinicalPearl: The alert line on the partograph is a referral trigger, not a treatment trigger; crossing it means the patient needs a higher level of care for evaluation.
keyClue: Cervical dilatation plot has crossed to the right of the alert line
commonExamTrap: Students often choose oxytocin augmentation when the alert line is crossed, but this decision requires specialist evaluation to exclude obstruction first.
missedConcept: The two-line system of the partograph: the alert line prompts referral, while the action line prompts active intervention.

clinicalStem: A 30-year-old G2P1 at 38 weeks of gestation is in active labour. The membranes rupture spontaneously and thick green meconium-stained amniotic fluid is noted. The midwife had been recording the fetal heart rate every 30 minutes as per protocol. What is the most appropriate change to fetal heart rate monitoring at this point?
options: Continue recording every 30 minutes as before
Record fetal heart rate every 15 minutes
Record fetal heart rate every 5 minutes
Proceed immediately to emergency caesarean section without further monitoring
Discontinue auscultation and apply a continuous cardiotocograph only
explanation: The partograph protocol states that when the amniotic fluid contains thick green or black meconium, the fetal heart rate should be counted every 5 minutes. Thick meconium is a sign of potential fetal compromise, and more frequent monitoring is required to detect early deceleration patterns. The routine 30-minute interval for the first stage is insufficient in this situation.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Continuing at 30-minute intervals is inadequate when thick meconium is present; this frequency does not allow timely detection of fetal compromise.
Fifteen-minute intervals are not the protocol specified for thick meconium; the partograph guidance specifies every 5 minutes.
Correct. The partograph specifically mandates fetal heart rate recording every 5 minutes when thick green or black meconium is present in the amniotic fluid.
Emergency caesarean section is not automatically indicated on the basis of meconium alone; immediate intensification of monitoring and senior review are the first steps.
A continuous cardiotocograph may be appropriate but is not the specific change dictated by the partograph protocol for meconium; the explicit instruction is auscultation every 5 minutes.
clinicalPearl: Thick meconium in the amniotic fluid mandates fetal heart rate auscultation every 5 minutes, as per partograph protocol — not the standard 30-minute interval.
keyClue: Thick green meconium-stained amniotic fluid noted after membrane rupture
commonExamTrap: Candidates confuse the routine first-stage interval (30 min) with the meconium-triggered interval (5 min); these are distinct protocol requirements.
missedConcept: Thick meconium is a fetal distress warning requiring immediate escalation of monitoring frequency on the partograph.

clinicalStem: A 22-year-old primigravida at 40 weeks of gestation has been in active labour for 10 hours. On vaginal examination the cervix is 8 cm dilated but on abdominal palpation 3/5 of the fetal head remains above the symphysis pubis. The examiner palpates the fetal skull sutures and finds that one parietal bone is overlapping another; when gentle pressure is applied to the overlapping bone it does not reduce. The partograph shows dilatation plots approaching the action line. What is the correct moulding grade and what does it signify?
options: Grade 0 moulding — normal finding, continue labour
Grade +1 moulding — sutures apposed but not overlapping, acceptable
Grade +2 moulding — overlapping but reducible, warrants closer observation
Grade +3 moulding — overlapping and non-reducible, indicates risk of obstructed labour
Grade +4 moulding — complete cranial overlap, immediate delivery required
explanation: Grade +3 moulding is defined as skull bones that are overlapping and the overlap is NOT reducible on gentle pressure. When +3 moulding is found together with poor progress of labour (inadequate head descent), this indicates increased risk of obstructed labour. The combination of +3 moulding, slow progress, and dilatation approaching the action line demands urgent senior obstetric review and likely operative delivery.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Grade 0 means bones are separated and sutures easily felt — not this case where overlap is non-reducible.
Grade +1 means adjacent bones are touching but not overlapping — not this case where there is non-reducible overlap.
Grade +2 means bones overlap but the overlap is reducible on gentle pressure — this case specifically states the overlap is NOT reducible.
Correct. Non-reducible overlapping of skull bones is grade +3, the most severe grade, and signals potential obstructed labour when accompanied by poor descent.
There is no grade +4 moulding in the partograph classification; the scale ends at +3.
clinicalPearl: Grade +3 moulding (non-reducible bone overlap) with slow head descent is an ominous sign of impending obstructed labour and requires immediate senior review.
keyClue: Overlapping skull bones that do not reduce on gentle pressure
commonExamTrap: Students confuse +2 (reducible overlap) with +3 (non-reducible overlap); the reducibility of the overlap is the critical distinguishing feature.
missedConcept: The moulding grading scale and its clinical significance: +3 non-reducible moulding combined with poor descent signals obstructed labour.

clinicalStem: A 28-year-old G1P0 at 39 weeks of gestation is assessed in active labour. On vaginal examination the cervix is 6 cm dilated. The midwife performs simultaneous abdominal palpation and reports that she cannot feel any part of the fetal head above the symphysis pubis. What should be recorded on the partograph for fetal descent, and what does this finding indicate?
options: Record descent as 5/5; indicates the head is not engaged
Record descent as 3/5; indicates partial engagement
Record descent as 1/5; indicates near-complete engagement
Record descent as 0/5; indicates the head is fully engaged
Descent cannot be recorded on the partograph by abdominal palpation alone
explanation: On the partograph, fetal descent is assessed by abdominal palpation and expressed in fifths of the head palpable above the symphysis pubis. If no part of the head is palpable above the symphysis (0/5), this means the head is fully descended (engaged) into the pelvis. A reading of 5/5 means the head is entirely above the symphysis and not engaged. Descent is plotted as a small 'O' at the same time as cervical dilatation.
wrongAnswerExplanations: 5/5 palpable above the symphysis means the head is completely above the pelvis and not engaged — the opposite of this case where nothing is felt.
3/5 palpable means only three-fifths of the head is still above the symphysis, indicating partial engagement — not the case described.
1/5 palpable means almost all of the head has descended and only a small portion remains above the symphysis — not the case described.
Correct. When no part of the head is palpable above the symphysis pubis, descent is recorded as 0/5, meaning fully descended and engaged.
Fetal descent is specifically assessed by abdominal palpation and recorded on the partograph alongside cervical dilatation; this is a core partograph parameter.
clinicalPearl: Fetal descent on the partograph is recorded in fifths above the symphysis: 5/5 = not engaged; 0/5 = fully engaged. It is assessed by abdominal palpation, not vaginal examination.
keyClue: Cannot feel any part of the fetal head above the symphysis pubis
commonExamTrap: Students confuse the direction of the scale: more fifths palpable above the symphysis means LESS descent, not more.
missedConcept: The partograph descent scale is inverse: higher numbers (5/5) mean the head is higher in the pelvis, while 0/5 means full engagement.

clinicalStem: A 32-year-old G3P2 at 38+3 weeks of gestation is admitted in active labour at 4 cm dilatation. A midwife is completing the partograph. She asks the supervising doctor how frequently she should record blood pressure, pulse, temperature, and uterine contractions. Which of the following correctly describes all four monitoring intervals on the partograph?
options: Blood pressure every 30 min; pulse every 30 min; temperature every 4 hours; contractions every 30 min
Blood pressure every 4 hours; pulse every 30 min; temperature every 2 hours; contractions every 30 min
Blood pressure every 2 hours; pulse every 30 min; temperature every 4 hours; contractions every hour
Blood pressure every 4 hours; pulse every hour; temperature every 2 hours; contractions every 15 min
Blood pressure every 4 hours; pulse every 30 min; temperature every 4 hours; contractions every 30 min
explanation: According to the partograph monitoring protocol: blood pressure is recorded every 4 hours; pulse is recorded every 30 minutes; temperature is recorded every 2 hours; and uterine contractions are assessed over 10 minutes and recorded every 30 minutes. Each of these intervals reflects the clinical priority of the parameter: temperature changes can signal infection within 2 hours, pulse detects maternal compromise every 30 minutes, and contractions (the engine of labour) are assessed every 30 minutes.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Blood pressure is not recorded every 30 minutes — the correct interval is every 4 hours. Temperature every 4 hours is also too infrequent; it should be every 2 hours.
Correct. Blood pressure: every 4 hours; pulse: every 30 minutes; temperature: every 2 hours; contractions: every 30 minutes — all intervals match the partograph protocol.
Blood pressure every 2 hours is not the protocol interval (it is every 4 hours); contractions should be every 30 minutes, not every hour.
Pulse every hour is too infrequent (should be every 30 minutes); contractions every 15 minutes is too frequent and not the standard protocol.
Temperature every 4 hours is incorrect; the partograph specifies temperature recording every 2 hours to detect early signs of infection.
clinicalPearl: Partograph monitoring intervals: pulse and contractions every 30 minutes; temperature every 2 hours; blood pressure every 4 hours.
keyClue: All four parameters asked: blood pressure, pulse, temperature, contractions
commonExamTrap: Temperature every 4 hours is frequently chosen but incorrect; the protocol specifies every 2 hours to detect intrapartum infection promptly.
missedConcept: Temperature must be recorded every 2 hours on the partograph — not every 4 hours — because intrapartum pyrexia from infection can develop rapidly.

clinicalStem: A 24-year-old primigravida at 40 weeks of gestation presents to the maternity unit with regular painful contractions. On vaginal examination her cervix is 3 cm dilated, 80% effaced, and the membranes are intact. The midwife asks when she should begin plotting cervical dilatation on the partograph. What is the correct answer?
options: Begin plotting immediately as she is already in early labour
Begin plotting when the cervix reaches 4 cm dilatation
Begin plotting when the membranes rupture
Begin plotting when contractions are at least 3 in 10 minutes
Begin plotting only when an epidural has been sited
explanation: The partograph protocol specifies that recording of cervical dilatation should begin only when the cervix reaches 4 cm dilatation, which marks the onset of the active phase of labour. At 3 cm the patient is still in the latent phase, and plotting too early may lead to misinterpretation of normal latent-phase progress as labour dystocia. If a woman arrives with a dilatation already beyond 4 cm (e.g. 6 cm), the first plot is placed on the alert line at the relevant centimetre mark.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Beginning at 3 cm is too early; the partograph is only started at 4 cm (onset of active phase) to avoid misinterpreting normal latent-phase progress.
Correct. The partograph is started when cervical dilatation reaches 4 cm, marking the beginning of the active phase of labour.
Membrane rupture is not the trigger for starting partograph recording; cervical dilatation reaching 4 cm is the trigger.
Contraction frequency alone is not the criterion for starting the partograph; active phase is defined by cervical dilatation of at least 4 cm.
Epidural siting has no bearing on when partograph recording should commence.
clinicalPearl: The partograph is started when cervical dilatation reaches 4 cm, marking the onset of the active phase; the latent phase is not plotted on the cervicograph.
keyClue: Cervix is 3 cm dilated — asking when to begin partograph
commonExamTrap: Students may choose to start the partograph immediately (at 3 cm) because the woman is in established contractions, but the rule is 4 cm for active phase.
missedConcept: The partograph active-phase plotting starts at 4 cm dilatation; plotting before this point will misrepresent normal latent-phase progress.

clinicalStem: A 34-year-old G2P1 at 39+5 weeks of gestation with a BMI of 38 and poorly controlled gestational diabetes is undergoing a vaginal delivery. The fetal head delivers normally but then retracts back against the maternal perineum and does not rotate externally. Gentle downward axial traction on the head fails to deliver the anterior shoulder. What is the correct diagnosis, and what sign is being demonstrated?
options: Cord prolapse — visible cord at the introitus is the warning sign
Uterine rupture — Bandl's ring is the warning sign
Shoulder dystocia — turtle sign is the warning sign
Brow presentation — absence of head rotation is the warning sign
Face presentation — failure of the chin to deliver is the warning sign
explanation: The clinical scenario describes shoulder dystocia: a vaginal cephalic delivery where the fetal head delivers but the shoulders cannot be delivered by gentle axial traction. The retraction of the delivered fetal head against the perineum (analogous to a turtle withdrawing into its shell) is the pathognomonic 'turtle sign'. This is a true obstetric emergency requiring immediate activation of the HELPER mnemonic.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Cord prolapse presents with visible or palpable cord at the introitus, often with sudden fetal bradycardia — not with the head already delivered and shoulder impaction.
Uterine rupture presents with abdominal pain, cessation of contractions, maternal shock, and fetal distress — not with shoulder impaction after head delivery.
Correct. Shoulder dystocia is defined by delivery requiring additional manoeuvres after the head is born; the turtle sign is the retraction of the delivered head against the perineum.
Brow presentation is a malpresentation diagnosed before or during head descent — the head has already delivered in this scenario.
Face presentation is diagnosed before delivery; failure of chin delivery is not the scenario described here.
clinicalPearl: The turtle sign — retraction of the delivered fetal head against the maternal perineum — is the cardinal warning sign of shoulder dystocia and demands immediate activation of the emergency protocol.
keyClue: Fetal head delivers then retracts against the perineum; gentle traction fails to deliver the shoulder
commonExamTrap: Students may think fundal pressure will help; fundal pressure is contraindicated in shoulder dystocia as it worsens impaction.
missedConcept: Shoulder dystocia is diagnosed clinically by failure of gentle traction to deliver the shoulders after head delivery; the turtle sign is the visual warning.
redFlagReason: Shoulder dystocia is an acute obstetric emergency: fetal hypoxia and acidosis develop within minutes of head-to-body delivery delay, risking hypoxic brain injury or neonatal death if not resolved promptly.

clinicalStem: A 29-year-old G1P0 at 41 weeks of gestation is delivering vaginally. After delivery of the fetal head, the turtle sign is recognised and the diagnosis of shoulder dystocia is made. The team activates the emergency protocol. As the first positional manoeuvre, what procedure is performed and what is its mechanism of action?
options: Place the mother in the knee-chest (all-fours) position to increase pelvic diameters by gravity
Apply firm fundal pressure to push the fetus downward into the pelvis
Flex the maternal hips sharply to 90 degrees against the abdomen (McRoberts manoeuvre), which straightens the lumbosacral lordosis and increases the anteroposterior pelvic diameter
Perform an immediate symphysiotomy to widen the bony pelvis
Deliver the posterior arm first by sweeping the fetal forearm across the chest
explanation: The McRoberts manoeuvre (the L = Legs step of HELPER) is the first positional manoeuvre for shoulder dystocia. The maternal hips are flexed to 90 degrees so that the thighs touch the sides of the abdomen. This straightens the lumbosacral lordosis, increases the anteroposterior diameter of the pelvis, and flexes the fetal spine, collectively helping to release the impacted anterior shoulder. It resolves approximately 42% of shoulder dystocia cases when combined with suprapubic pressure.
wrongAnswerExplanations: The all-fours (Gaskin) position is a later manoeuvre in the HELPER sequence (R3 = Roll); it is not the first positional manoeuvre.
Fundal pressure is specifically contraindicated in shoulder dystocia because it drives the shoulder further into the symphysis and worsens impaction.
Correct. McRoberts manoeuvre is the first positional step: sharp hip flexion to 90 degrees straightens the lumbosacral lordosis and increases the AP pelvic diameter, resolving about 42% of cases.
Symphysiotomy is a last-resort procedure used only when all standard manoeuvres have failed; it is never the first step.
Delivery of the posterior arm is an effective but later manoeuvre in the HELPER sequence (R1 = Removal), not the first positional step.
clinicalPearl: McRoberts manoeuvre (hip flexion to 90 degrees) resolves approximately 42% of shoulder dystocia cases and is always the first positional manoeuvre — combined immediately with suprapubic pressure.
keyClue: First positional manoeuvre in shoulder dystocia management
commonExamTrap: Fundal pressure is frequently and incorrectly chosen; it is absolutely contraindicated in shoulder dystocia.
missedConcept: McRoberts works by straightening the lumbosacral spine and increasing the AP pelvic diameter, not by widening the lateral diameter.
redFlagReason: Shoulder dystocia is a time-critical emergency; each minute of delay increases the risk of fetal acidosis, hypoxic brain injury, and neonatal death. Correct immediate management is life-saving.

clinicalStem: A 38-year-old G3P2 attends antenatal clinic at 36 weeks of gestation. Her BMI is 42, HbA1c is 8.2%, she has had a shoulder dystocia in her previous pregnancy, and the current estimated fetal weight on ultrasound is 4.2 kg. She asks about her risk of shoulder dystocia in this delivery. Which combination of factors most significantly increases her risk?
options: Advanced maternal age and grand multiparity alone are the main risk factors
Macrosomia, poorly controlled diabetes, maternal obesity, and previous shoulder dystocia
Post-dates pregnancy and use of epidural analgesia
Previous lower-segment caesarean section and macrosomia
Polyhydramnios and breech presentation in a previous pregnancy
explanation: The recognised risk factors for shoulder dystocia include macrosomia (estimated fetal weight >4 kg), poorly controlled gestational or insulin-dependent diabetes (which promotes fetal macrosomia with disproportionate shoulder fat deposition), maternal obesity, and a previous delivery complicated by shoulder dystocia (recurrence rate 10-15%). This patient carries all four major risk factors simultaneously, placing her at high risk. Careful planning of mode of delivery is essential.
wrongAnswerExplanations: While advanced maternal age and multiparity can be associated with larger babies, they are not the primary independent risk factors listed in the shoulder dystocia lecture; the four key risk factors are macrosomia, diabetes, obesity, and previous shoulder dystocia.
Correct. Macrosomia, poorly controlled diabetes, maternal obesity, and previous shoulder dystocia are the four major risk factors enumerated in the lecture, and this patient has all four.
Post-dates pregnancy may contribute to macrosomia, but epidural analgesia is not a primary risk factor for shoulder dystocia.
Previous caesarean section is not a risk factor for shoulder dystocia; it is usually protective against it if repeat caesarean is chosen.
Polyhydramnios and previous breech presentation are not listed as primary risk factors for shoulder dystocia.
clinicalPearl: The four major modifiable/predictable risk factors for shoulder dystocia are macrosomia, poorly controlled diabetes, maternal obesity, and a previous shoulder dystocia (recurrence rate 10-15%).
keyClue: BMI 42, HbA1c 8.2%, previous shoulder dystocia, estimated fetal weight 4.2 kg
commonExamTrap: More than 50% of shoulder dystocia cases occur in normal-birthweight infants, so macrosomia is a risk factor but is not required for the diagnosis.
missedConcept: Shoulder dystocia recurrence rate is 10-15%; a previous shoulder dystocia should always trigger careful mode-of-delivery planning for subsequent pregnancies.

clinicalStem: A term neonate is delivered after a difficult shoulder dystocia. On neonatal examination the infant's right arm hangs limply at the side, internally rotated, with the forearm pronated — the classic 'waiter's tip' posture. There is paralysis of the deltoid, biceps, and brachialis muscles but hand grip is intact. Which nerve roots are injured, and what is the diagnosis?
options: C8-T1 nerve roots — Klumpke's palsy
C5-C6 nerve roots — Erb's palsy
C7-C8 nerve roots — total brachial plexus palsy
C3-C4 nerve roots — phrenic nerve palsy
C5-T1 nerve roots — Klumpke's palsy
explanation: The 'waiter's tip' posture with paralysis of the deltoid, biceps, and brachialis muscles but preserved hand grip is the classic presentation of Erb's palsy, caused by injury to the C5-C6 nerve roots. This is the most common brachial plexus injury following shoulder dystocia and results from excessive lateral traction on the neck, stretching the upper trunk of the brachial plexus. Klumpke's palsy involves C8-T1 and presents with loss of intrinsic hand muscles.
wrongAnswerExplanations: C8-T1 injury causes Klumpke's palsy, which presents with wrist and finger weakness and loss of intrinsic hand function — not 'waiter's tip' with preserved grip.
Correct. C5-C6 injury causes Erb's palsy: paralysis of deltoid, biceps, and brachialis with preserved hand grip; the arm hangs in internal rotation with forearm pronation.
C7-C8 injury affects triceps and wrist extensors; total brachial plexus palsy involves the entire arm including the hand — not the pattern described.
C3-C4 injury affects the phrenic nerve causing hemidiaphragm palsy with respiratory symptoms — not the arm paralysis described.
C5-T1 injury would constitute a total brachial plexus injury with hand involvement — grip would not be intact in this scenario.
clinicalPearl: Erb's palsy (C5-C6) is the most common neonatal brachial plexus injury after shoulder dystocia; it causes the 'waiter's tip' posture with preserved hand grip because C8-T1 (hand intrinsics) are unaffected.
keyClue: Waiter's tip posture, paralysis of deltoid/biceps/brachialis, preserved hand grip
commonExamTrap: Students confuse Erb's (C5-C6, upper trunk, waiter's tip) with Klumpke's (C8-T1, lower trunk, claw hand); the preserved grip in this case confirms C8-T1 are intact.
missedConcept: Brachial plexus injury after shoulder dystocia: Erb's palsy (C5-C6) = waiter's tip; Klumpke's palsy (C8-T1) = claw hand.

clinicalStem: A 31-year-old G2P1 at 39 weeks of gestation is in the second stage of labour. After delivery of the fetal head, shoulder dystocia is recognised and the HELPER protocol is activated. The McRoberts manoeuvre has been applied. An assistant is now asked to apply suprapubic pressure (P = Pressure). Which of the following correctly describes the technique and direction of suprapubic pressure in this situation?
options: Apply pressure directly downward onto the fundus to push the fetus into the pelvis
Apply firm pressure in a rocking motion directed upward above the symphysis to dislodge the posterior shoulder
Apply pressure using a locking hand position above the symphysis, directing force to adduct the anterior shoulder toward the fetal chest, initially continuous then rocking
Apply bimanual compression — one hand suprapubically and the other intravaginally — in a squeezing motion
Apply lateral pressure to both sides of the uterus simultaneously to rotate the fetal torso
explanation: Suprapubic pressure is applied by an assistant using a locking hand position (similar to CPR) placed just above the symphysis pubis. The force is directed to adduct (not laterally displace) the anterior shoulder, reducing its breadth and facilitating entry into the oblique pelvic diameter. The assistant initially applies continuous pressure for 30-60 seconds; if ineffective, a rocking motion is then used. Fundal pressure is absolutely contraindicated as it drives the shoulder further into the symphysis.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Fundal pressure is absolutely contraindicated in shoulder dystocia; it worsens impaction by pushing the anterior shoulder harder against the symphysis pubis.
Pressure directed upward above the symphysis would not adduct the anterior shoulder and is not the described technique; direction must be toward the fetal chest to adduct the shoulder.

Correct. Suprapubic pressure uses a CPR-like locking hand position just above the symphysis, force directed to adduct the anterior shoulder, initially continuous for 30-60 seconds then rocking if needed.
Bimanual compression is used for postpartum haemorrhage (PPH) management — it has no role in the management of shoulder dystocia.
Lateral uterine compression is not a described manoeuvre for shoulder dystocia and would not address the bony shoulder impaction.
clinicalPearl: Suprapubic pressure in shoulder dystocia must be directed to ADDUCT the anterior shoulder (pushing it toward the fetal chest), not downward — and fundal pressure must never be used.
keyClue: P = Pressure step of HELPER; anterior shoulder impacted at symphysis
commonExamTrap: Fundal pressure is the single most commonly chosen wrong answer for shoulder dystocia management questions; it is absolutely contraindicated.
missedConcept: The mechanism of suprapubic pressure: adduction of the anterior shoulder reduces the shoulder-to-shoulder diameter, allowing it to slip into the oblique diameter of the pelvis.
redFlagReason: Applying fundal pressure (a common error) in shoulder dystocia worsens impaction and can cause fetal hypoxia, neonatal death, or uterine rupture — distinguishing the correct technique is clinically critical.

subtopic: Zavanelli manoeuvre and last resort options
clinicalStem: A 36-year-old G1P0 at 40 weeks of gestation with a BMI of 45 and macrosomia (estimated fetal weight 4.6 kg) has a shoulder dystocia. The McRoberts manoeuvre, suprapubic pressure, internal rotational manoeuvres (Rubin II, Woods screw), removal of the posterior arm, and the all-fours (Gaskin) position have all been attempted without success. The baby has not been delivered and the elapsed head-to-body time is 8 minutes. Which of the following is classified as a last-resort option?
options: Repeat McRoberts manoeuvre combined with suprapubic pressure
Gaskin (all-fours) manoeuvre with delivery of the superior shoulder
Zavanelli manoeuvre — cephalic replacement followed by emergency caesarean delivery
Application of Kjelland's forceps to rotate the fetal head
Administration of oxytocin to augment contractions and push the shoulder free
explanation: The Zavanelli manoeuvre is a last-resort procedure for irreducible shoulder dystocia: the delivered fetal head is flexed and replaced into the vagina (cephalic replacement), followed by immediate emergency caesarean delivery. It requires analgesia/anaesthesia, an operative team, and uterine relaxation (tocolysis). It is contraindicated if the umbilical cord has already been clamped and cut. Other last-resort options include symphysiotomy and abdominal rescue (deliberate hysterotomy).
wrongAnswerExplanations: Repeating McRoberts with suprapubic pressure is appropriate early in the HELPER sequence; at 8 minutes with all standard manoeuvres exhausted, this is not a new last-resort option.
The Gaskin (all-fours) manoeuvre has already been attempted; it is part of the standard sequence (R3), not a last-resort option.
Correct. The Zavanelli manoeuvre — cephalic replacement and emergency caesarean — is explicitly classified as a last-resort method when all standard HELPER manoeuvres have failed.
Kjelland's forceps are a rotational forceps instrument used for malposition of the head before delivery, not for resolving shoulder dystocia after head delivery.
Oxytocin augmentation would increase fundal pressure and worsen shoulder impaction; it is absolutely contraindicated in shoulder dystocia.
clinicalPearl: Last-resort options for refractory shoulder dystocia are the Zavanelli manoeuvre (cephalic replacement + emergency caesarean), symphysiotomy, and abdominal rescue — all requiring anaesthesia and an operative team.
keyClue: All standard HELPER manoeuvres exhausted at 8 minutes elapsed time
commonExamTrap: Students may choose oxytocin for augmentation, forgetting it is contraindicated (increases fundal pressure and worsens impaction); others confuse Zavanelli with a routine manoeuvre.
missedConcept: Zavanelli requires uterine relaxation (tocolysis) and is not possible if the umbilical cord has been clamped and divided.
redFlagReason: Eight minutes of shoulder dystocia carries severe risk of fetal acidosis (pH <7), hypoxic brain injury, and neonatal death; correct escalation to last-resort options is immediately life-saving.
